mechanism_explained: Cell cycle as a checkpoint-gated decision system (cyclin-CDK, p53, Rb); losing one control protein lets ordinary DNA damage propagate.
Hallmarks as acquired capabilities; grade-vs-stage and benign-vs-malignant clinical translation.
Causal inference (Bradford Hill) separating causal confidence from effect size; exposure to molecular damage to prevention.
Cancer as clonal evolution: driver/passenger selection, Knudson two-hit logic, DNA-repair failure as accelerant.
A proto-oncogene becomes an oncogene when one dominant gain-of-function lesion locks a growth-signaling protein active.
Tumor suppressors are brakes needing biallelic loss; loss removes restraint but creates synthetic-lethal dependencies.
Cancer alters which genes are read via DNA methylation and the histone code without changing sequence; reversible, druggable.
Noncoding RNAs and Polycomb/Trithorax reprogramming drive plasticity, identity change, and therapy resistance.
Carcinogens introduce DNA damage that becomes exposure-specific mutational signatures (procarcinogen to adduct to mutation).
Persistent infection causes cancer via viral oncoproteins, integration, or chronic inflammation; preventable by vaccination/eradication.
Cyclin-CDK complexes drive a conditional cycle gated by the bistable Rb-E2F switch and three checkpoints.
Cancers force the Rb-E2F gate open; downstream Rb-pathway integrity decides CDK4/6-inhibitor response and resistance.
BCL-2 family balance governs MOMP, the irreversible commitment to caspase-driven death.
Cancers suppress their own death program; BH3 mimetics (venetoclax) displace a guardian from BAX/BAK to restore death.
Aerobic glycolysis is a carbon-allocation strategy for biomass; HIF-1a/VHL drives pseudohypoxic glycolysis.
Rewired lipid/amino-acid metabolism creates dependencies (glutamine, MTAP-PRMT5 synthetic lethality) in a hostile microenvironment.
